back: Vertex connectivity κ(G) is the minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph or reduces it to a trivial graph (single vertex).

front: What is a Cut Vertex (Articulation Point)?
back: A cut vertex is a vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components in the graph. It is essential for maintaining graph connectivity.
A graph is biconnected (2-vertex-connected) if it contains no cut vertices. It remains connected after removal of any single vertex.
A block is a maximal biconnected subgraph. It is a subgraph that cannot be made more biconnected by adding edges.

front: State Menger's Theorem for Vertex Connectivity
back: The maximum number of internally disjoint paths between two vertices equals the minimum number of vertices separating them.
A vertex set S is a vertex cut if removing S disconnects the graph or makes it trivial. The size of the smallest vertex cut is κ(G).
A bipartite tree where block nodes represent biconnected components and cut vertices are connected to the blocks containing them.

front: Relationship between κ(G), λ(G), and δ(G)
back: κ(G) ≤ λ(G) ≤ δ(G), where κ is vertex connectivity, λ is edge connectivity, and δ is minimum degree.

front: Vertex Connectivity of Complete Graph Kₙ
back: κ(Kₙ) = n-1. Removing all n-1 vertices leaves an empty graph, so minimum cut size is n-1.
Paths between two vertices are internally disjoint if they share no common vertices except the endpoints.
A separating set (vertex separator) is a set of vertices whose removal separates two specified vertices or disconnects the graph.
A graph is 2-vertex-connected if it has no cut vertices and remains connected after removal of any single vertex.

front: How to find Cut Vertices using DFS?
back: In DFS tree, a vertex v (not root) is a cut vertex if it has a child u where no back edge from subtree of u reaches ancestors of v. Root is cut vertex if it has ≥2 children.

front: Define Connectivity in terms of Disconnection
back: A graph is k-vertex-connected if it has more than k vertices and remains connected after removal of any k-1 vertices.

front: What is a Bridge in vertex connectivity context?
back: An edge whose removal increases the number of connected components. Bridges are edges, not vertices, but relate to connectivity.
